perturbation.original_text: to return or destroy or if stored in electronic form to erase from its systems (to the extent possible) at its own expe nse the Confidential Infor mation of the Disclosing Party together with any copies in its possession or in the possession of its Representatives at the request of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: to return or destroy or if stored in electronic form to erase from its systems (to the extent possible) at its own expense the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party together with any copies in its possession or in the possession of its Representatives only if the Disclosing Party makes such a request within 90 days of termination of the agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the return/destruction requirement to only apply if requested within 90 days of termination creates a conflict with general legal principles regarding trade secret protection, which often extend beyond a short, fixed period after contract termination. This short deadline undermines the purpose of confidentiality agreements, especially for long-term trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 provides broad protection for trade secrets, and limiting the request for return/destruction to 90 days may be deemed insufficient to protect those secrets, especially in industries where trade secrets have a longer lifespan. This creates a potential conflict because the law provides recourse for misappropriation, whereas the contract limits the timeframe for action.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 90-day limit for requesting the return or destruction of confidential information, which contradicts the broad protection afforded to trade secrets under 18 U.S. Code § 1836. While the snippet provided does not explicitly state a timeframe for trade secret protection, the general principle is that this protection extends for as long as the information remains a trade secret. The original text aligns with this principle by requiring return or destruction 'at the request of the Disclosing Party,' without setting a time limit. By contrast, the modified text limits this right to 90 days after termination, potentially undermining the protection of trade secrets with longer lifespans. The law snippet notes, 'An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce,' suggesting a broader scope than the contractual limitation.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or di scussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of five (5) years from the termination of this A greement.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or di scussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of ninety (90) days from the termination of this A greement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to 90 days contradicts the reasonable duration typically expected for trade secret protection, creating a legal inconsistency.
perturbation.law_explanation: The legal protection for trade secrets often extends far beyond 90 days, depending on the nature of the secret and the industry. By limiting the confidentiality obligations to such a short period, the contract fails to provide adequate protection as envisioned by trade secret law, potentially rendering it unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a five-year confidentiality period, while the changed contract reduces this to 90 days. The Law Explanation states that trade secret protection often extends far beyond 90 days. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 USC 1836) pertains to the protection of trade secrets and civil actions related to their misappropriation. Although the snippet doesn't specify a minimum duration for trade secret protection, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest a 90-day period is insufficient for reasonable trade secret protection, potentially contradicting the intent of laws protecting trade secrets as described in the explanations. Thus, reducing the confidentiality period creates a contradiction with the need to adequately protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice required to be given under t his Agreement, shall be in writing and shall be delivered personally, or sent by pre -paid first class post or recorded delivery or by commercial courier, to each Party required to receive the notice at its address as set out above or as otherwise specified by the relevant Party by notice in writing to each other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice required to be given under this Agreement, shall be delivered exclusively by carrier pigeon to each Party required to receive the notice at its address as set out above or as otherwise specified by the relevant Party by notice in writing to each other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the notification method to 'carrier pigeon' is not a 'commercially reasonable' means of communication. This is especially troublesome in international contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UETA provides that, among other things, electronic notifications are valid. The requirement that all notices be delivered by 'carrier pigeon' contradicts the UETA, which supports the use of modern electronic communication methods, and could be deemed unreasonable.

perturbation.justification: The original text allows for multiple methods of delivering notice, including commercially reasonable options. The changed text limits notification exclusively to 'carrier pigeon.' The Perturbation Explanation notes that this is not a 'commercially reasonable' means of communication, especially in international contracts. The Law Explanation states that the UETA supports the use of modern electronic communication methods. While the provided law snippets are not directly relevant, the explanations provided support the determination that limiting communication to only 'carrier pigeon' contradicts the UETA's support for modern communication methods, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The parties irr evocably agree that the courts o f Ireland shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.changed_text: The parties irrevocably agree that all disputes or claims arising out of or in connection with this agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non-contractual disputes or claims) shall be resolved through binding arbitration conducted by a random stranger selected from a phone book, whose decision shall be final and non-appealable.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing court jurisdiction with arbitration by a 'random stranger' is unenforceable and contradicts standard arbitration practices. This creates a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act outlines grounds for vacating an arbitration award, including arbitrator misconduct or bias. The 'random stranger' clause introduces inherent bias and lack of qualification, contradicting the FAA's requirement for fair and impartial arbitration. The fact that there would be no appeals allowed is also contradictory to the FAA.

Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration- (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon s
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces court jurisdiction with arbitration by a 'random stranger,' which contradicts standard arbitration practices and introduces inherent bias. The Law Explanation argues this violates the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) due to the arbitrator's lack of qualification and impartiality, and because no appeals are allowed. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 9 U.S. Code § 10, outlines grounds for vacating an arbitration award, including 'evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators.' Selecting an arbitrator randomly from a phone book suggests a high likelihood of partiality or lack of competence, directly contradicting the FAA's implicit requirement for a fair and qualified arbitrator. Furthermore, the clause stating the decision shall be 'final and non-appealable' clashes with the judicial oversight implied in 9 U.S. Code § 10, where a court can vacate an award under specific conditions. This change not only weakens but directly opposes the spirit and potential recourse mechanisms within established arbitration frameworks.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with Irish law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of North Korea.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying North Korean law may be unenforceable due to lack of familiarity, access to legal resources, and potential conflicts with fundamental fairness and public policy in jurisdictions where the contract is likely to be enforced (e.g., Ireland or the United States).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: Applying North Korean law to a contract between an Irish company and another entity, especially if that entity is based in a country with significantly different legal principles (like the US) may violate the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment, as it could deprive a party of a fair hearing due to the inaccessibility and potential incompatibility of that legal system.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Fifth Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Fifth Amendment The Fifth Amendment creates a number of rights relevant to both criminal and civil legal proceedings. In criminal cases , the Fifth Amendment guarantees the right to a grand jury , forbids “ double jeopardy ,” and protects against self-incrimination . It also requires that “ due process of law ” be part of any proceeding that denies a citizen “life, liberty or property” and requires the government to compensate citizens when it takes private property for public use. Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment V No person shall be held to answer for a capital, or otherwise infamous crime, unless on a presentment or indictment of a grand jury, except in cases arising in the land or naval forces, or in the militia, when in actual service in time of war or public danger; nor shall any person be subject for the same offense to be twice put in jeopardy of life or limb; nor shall be compelled in any criminal case to be a witness against himself, nor be deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law; nor shall private property be taken for public use, without just compensation. Wex Resources Fifth Amendment Criminal Law / Criminal Procedure Due Process Substantive Due Process Miranda Warning Indictment Privilege Against Self-Incrimination Self-Incrimination Grand Jury Jury Double Jeopardy Collateral Estoppel Immunity From Prosecution Eminent Domain Takings Territorial Jurisdiction Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Fourth Amendment up Sixth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces Irish law with North Korean law as the governing law of the contract. The Law Explanation states this may violate the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines the Fifth Amendment as guaranteeing "due process of law". Applying North Korean law to parties likely based elsewhere, as suggested by the Perturbation Explanation (Ireland or the United States), undermines this right because North Korean law may be inaccessible or incompatible, thus failing to provide due process.
